answers.field_label: These Are The Seizure Medicines That Are Currently Being Taken
answers.value:
Currently taking:

1) Levetiracetam (Keppra) 750 mg by mouth twice daily (7:00 AM and 7:00 PM)
2) Clobazam (Onfi) 10 mg by mouth in the morning and 20 mg by mouth at bedtime
3) Lamotrigine (Lamictal) 150 mg by mouth twice daily
4) Rescue medication: Intranasal midazolam (Nayzilam) 5 mg as needed for seizures lasting longer than 5 minutes or for 3 or more seizures within 1 hour (used about 1–2 times per month)

No known drug allergies. No recent dose changes in the last 3 months.

answers.field_label: These Are The Types Of Seizures That Are Occurring While On These Medicines
answers.value:
Despite the current medications, the following seizure types are still occurring:

1) Focal impaired-awareness seizures (most common):
   - Frequency: About 1–2 per week on average. Some weeks there are none, but other weeks there can be 3–4.
   - Description: The patient suddenly stops what they are doing, stares blankly, and becomes unresponsive. Sometimes they make chewing or lip-smacking movements and pick at their clothes with their right hand. These episodes usually last 30–90 seconds.
   - After-effects: Confusion and disorientation for about 10–20 minutes afterward, followed by fatigue and headache. Often needs to lie down and may fall asleep.

2) Focal to bilateral tonic–clonic seizures (generalized convulsions):
   - Frequency: Roughly 1–2 per month, often clustering around times of illness, stress, or sleep deprivation.
   - Description: Often starts with a brief aura (feeling of déjà vu, rising sensation in the stomach, and a sense that “something bad is about to happen”), then loss of awareness, followed by stiffening and rhythmic jerking of all four limbs. There is sometimes a loud cry at onset. These seizures usually last 1–3 minutes.
   - After-effects: Postictal confusion, headache, sore muscles, and extreme fatigue lasting several hours. Occasionally there is tongue biting and urinary incontinence.

3) Brief myoclonic jerks:
   - Frequency: A few times per week, mostly in the morning shortly after waking.
   - Description: Sudden, quick jerks of the arms and sometimes shoulders that can cause the patient to drop objects like a toothbrush or coffee mug. Awareness is preserved during these events.

No history of absence (staring) spells with immediate recovery or atonic (drop) attacks. Seizures are more common when the patient is sick with a cold, has not slept well, or has missed a dose of medication. There have been two emergency room visits in the last 6 months due to prolonged tonic–clonic seizures that did not stop quickly with the rescue medication.

answers.field_label: My Goals For Seizure Treatment Are
answers.value:
My main goals for seizure treatment, in order of priority, are:

1) Significant reduction in seizure frequency and severity:
   - Ideally, I would like to get to the point where I am having no tonic–clonic seizures at all, and at most only occasional focal impaired-awareness seizures (less than one per month).
   - Right now, I am having 1–2 tonic–clonic seizures per month and 1–2 focal impaired-awareness seizures per week, which is affecting my safety, independence, and ability to work. A realistic short-term goal for me would be at least a 50–75% reduction in overall seizure frequency within the next 6–12 months.

2) Better day-to-day functioning and independence:
   - I would like to be able to reliably attend work and social activities without constantly worrying about having a seizure in public.
   - I currently avoid going places alone (like grocery shopping or walking the dog) because of the fear of collapsing or becoming confused around strangers. I would like to reach a point where outings on my own feel safe and routine.

3) Improving quality of life and reducing medication side effects:
   - On my current medications, I experience a lot of fatigue, brain fog, and irritability, especially with Keppra and clobazam. I sometimes struggle with memory (like forgetting conversations or misplacing items) and have difficulty concentrating on tasks at my job (I work part-time as an office assistant).
   - My goal is to find a treatment plan that controls seizures more effectively without making me feel sedated, emotionally flat, or “not myself.” I would like to feel more alert in the mornings and less drowsy in the afternoons.

4) Safety and fewer emergency situations:
   - I want to reduce the number of times I need to use rescue medication and avoid emergency room visits altogether if possible.
   - My family and I would like to feel more confident that we can manage my seizures at home and that prolonged or cluster seizures will be much less likely.

5) Long-term stability and planning for the future:
   - I’m 27 years old and would like to eventually be able to drive again if it becomes safe and legally allowed (once I meet the seizure-free requirements in my state).
   - I also want to be able to consider future plans like moving into my own apartment and possibly starting a family, which currently feels out of reach with the level of seizure activity I have now.

Overall, my main goal is to reduce seizures enough that I can live more independently, feel safe in my daily life, and participate more fully in work, relationships, and activities I enjoy, without feeling so limited by my condition or the side effects of my current medications.

answers.value:
I understand that EPIDIOLEX is primarily approved for certain types of epilepsy, but my neurologist and I are discussing whether it might be appropriate for my situation. My hopes for EPIDIOLEX are:

1) Fewer and less severe seizures:
   - I am hoping that adding EPIDIOLEX will significantly reduce both my focal impaired-awareness seizures and my tonic–clonic seizures.
   - I would be very happy if EPIDIOLEX could cut my tonic–clonic seizures down from 1–2 per month to maybe only a few per year, or even eliminate them entirely.
   - I am also hoping that the morning myoclonic jerks will decrease so I can start my day more normally and not worry about dropping things or injuring myself.

2) Better tolerance and fewer cognitive/emotional side effects compared with my current medications:
   - Some of my current seizure medications make me feel tired, foggy, and more irritable. My hope is that EPIDIOLEX either has milder side effects for me personally or allows us to lower the doses of some of my other medications over time.
   - I have read that some people feel more clear-headed on EPIDIOLEX compared with other drugs. I am hoping that if it works for my seizures, I might eventually be able to reduce the dose of clobazam or Keppra, which seem to be contributing to mood changes and fatigue.

3) More stable mood and improved overall well-being:
   - Living with frequent seizures has taken a toll on my mental health. I’m not expecting EPIDIOLEX to be an antidepressant, but better seizure control would likely reduce my anxiety and improve my confidence.
   - If EPIDIOLEX helps stabilize my seizure pattern, I’m hoping I will feel less on edge and less fearful about when the next big seizure will happen.

4) Better sleep and more predictable days:
   - When I have seizures at night or very early in the morning, my entire day is disrupted. I’m hoping that if EPIDIOLEX helps control seizures, my sleep will be less interrupted and I will have fewer days lost to postictal fatigue.

5) A step toward long-term goals:
   - If EPIDIOLEX helps bring my seizures under better control, I hope it will move me closer to being eligible to drive again in the future (depending on seizure-free intervals and my state’s regulations).
   - I also hope it will allow me to take on more hours at work without worrying that I will have to call out or leave early due to seizures or medication side effects.

I know there are potential side effects, including effects on the liver and possible interactions with my other seizure medications. I am willing to have regular blood work and follow-up visits to monitor this. Overall, my hope is that EPIDIOLEX can be an additional tool that makes a meaningful difference in my seizure control and quality of life, even if it does not completely eliminate seizures.

answers.field_label: Additional Notes And Questions I Have
answers.value:
Additional notes:
- I was first diagnosed with epilepsy at age 15 after a tonic–clonic seizure at school. I have tried several medications over the years (including topiramate and valproic acid), but these were stopped due to side effects (significant weight loss, cognitive slowing, and hair loss with valproic acid).
- I currently live with my older sister, who helps monitor my seizures and makes sure I take my medications on time. She is very supportive but also works full-time, so we are both hoping for a treatment plan that makes my seizures less unpredictable.
- I do not drink alcohol or use recreational drugs. I drink 1–2 cups of coffee per day. I try to maintain a regular sleep schedule, but seizures and medication side effects sometimes interfere.
- I am not currently pregnant and not planning pregnancy in the immediate future, but I would like to know how EPIDIOLEX might affect future family planning.

Questions I would like to discuss with my neurologist:
1) Appropriateness of EPIDIOLEX for my specific seizure type:
   - Given that my seizures are primarily focal with secondary generalization, how strong is the evidence that EPIDIOLEX might help in my particular case?
   - Is this considered an off-label use, and how does that affect insurance coverage and monitoring?

2) Interactions with my current medications:
   - How will EPIDIOLEX interact with clobazam, Keppra, and lamotrigine? I have read that it can increase clobazam levels.
   - Will we need to adjust the dose of clobazam or other medications as we increase the EPIDIOLEX dose?

3) Side effects and monitoring:
   - What are the most common side effects I should watch for (e.g., sleepiness, diarrhea, changes in appetite, mood changes)?
   - How often will I need blood tests to check my liver function or medication levels, especially at the start and as we adjust the dose?

4) Dosing and expectations:
   - How quickly would we start EPIDIOLEX and increase the dose? Over what time frame should I expect to see whether it is helping?
   - What would be considered a “successful” response (for example, 50% reduction in seizures), and at what point would we decide to continue, adjust, or stop the medication if it is not working well enough?

5) Impact on daily activities and driving:
   - If EPIDIOLEX works and my seizures decrease significantly, how long would I need to be seizure-free before we could discuss driving again according to my state’s regulations?
   - Are there any restrictions related specifically to EPIDIOLEX (for example, increased drowsiness) that might affect work or operating machinery?

6) Practical issues:
   - Will my insurance likely cover EPIDIOLEX for my type of epilepsy, or will we need prior authorization or documentation of previous medication trials?
   - Is there a patient assistance program or cost support available if coverage is limited?

Overall, I am open to trying EPIDIOLEX because I feel that my current seizure control is not where it needs to be, and the side effects of my existing medications are impacting my quality of life. I want to understand the potential benefits and risks as clearly as possible so I can make an informed decision together with my neurologist and my family.

persona.background_context:
This is Emily, a 27-year-old woman living in a two-bedroom apartment in the Clintonville neighborhood of Columbus, Ohio, with her older sister, Hannah, and their scruffy rescue dog, Oscar. Emily was first diagnosed with epilepsy at 15 after a tonic–clonic seizure in the hallway at her high school in Dayton, an event that still shapes how she thinks about safety and independence. Over the years she has tried several medications, including topiramate and valproic acid, but had to stop them because of side effects like significant weight loss, cognitive slowing, and hair loss. Currently, she takes levetiracetam (Keppra) 750 mg twice a day at 7:00 AM and 7:00 PM, clobazam (Onfi) 10 mg in the morning and 20 mg at bedtime, and lamotrigine (Lamictal) 150 mg twice daily. She keeps a box with blister packs laid out on the kitchen counter next to the coffee maker so she and Hannah can both see if doses have been taken. For rescue, there’s intranasal midazolam (Nayzilam) 5 mg in her crossbody bag and another in the nightstand, used about once or twice a month when a seizure lasts longer than five minutes or when she clusters. She has no known drug allergies.

Despite this regimen, Emily still has frequent seizures. Most weeks she has one or two focal impaired-awareness seizures—episodes where she suddenly stops what she’s doing, stares blankly, and becomes unresponsive, sometimes making chewing movements or picking at her clothes with her right hand for 30–90 seconds. Afterward she often feels confused and disoriented for 10–20 minutes, then exhausted with a pounding headache, usually needing to lie down. About once or twice a month she has focal to bilateral tonic–clonic seizures, often when she’s under extra stress, fighting off a cold, or hasn’t slept well. Those start with a familiar aura: a rush of déjà vu, a rising sensation in her stomach, and the ominous feeling that “something bad is about to happen,” followed by loss of awareness and full-body convulsions. These events have led to two emergency room visits in the past six months at OhioHealth Riverside Methodist Hospital when seizures didn’t stop quickly with the rescue medication. She also has brief myoclonic jerks a few mornings each week, usually within an hour of waking, which can cause her to fling her toothbrush or drop her coffee mug, though she remains fully aware during them. She doesn’t drink alcohol or use recreational drugs, and she keeps caffeine to one or two cups of coffee a day, trying to guard her sleep as much as possible.

Emily works about 20–24 hours a week as a part-time office assistant at Riverside Family Dental Clinic, a small practice near the Olentangy River. She usually works Monday, Wednesday, and Friday from 9:30 AM to 3:00 PM so she can avoid the most chaotic commuter traffic and have time in the morning to fully wake up through the fogginess and occasional myoclonic jerks. Her job involves answering phones, scheduling appointments in their practice management software, scanning insurance cards, and filing charts. She used to take pride in being extremely organized, but the combination of seizures and medication side effects has made it harder to concentrate and remember details. She occasionally forgets to relay a phone message or double-books an appointment, which frustrates her because she knows she’s capable of more. Her coworkers are generally understanding—her manager, Denise, has a daughter with migraines and is sympathetic—but Emily still worries about being seen as unreliable, especially on days she has to call out or leave early after a seizure.

She completed two years toward an associate degree in administrative office technology at Columbus State Community College but took a medical leave at 21 after a series of breakthrough seizures and never went back full-time. She sometimes scrolls through the college website, looking at evening and online classes, imagining finishing her degree once her seizures are better controlled. Her neurologist at the Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center, Dr. Arjun Mehta, has been working with her since she moved to Columbus in 2021. At her last appointment in January 2026, after reviewing her seizure diary and discussing how exhausted and “not herself” she feels on her current combination of Keppra, clobazam, and lamotrigine, they began talking seriously about adding Epidiolex (cannabidiol). Emily has done her own reading about it, understands it’s approved for certain epilepsy syndromes and that using it for her focal seizures would be off-label, and is cautiously hopeful it could reduce her seizure frequency and allow for lower doses of some of her current medications over time.

At home, daily life revolves around balancing independence with safety. Hannah, who is 32 and works full-time as a project coordinator for a nonprofit in downtown Columbus, usually leaves the apartment around 8:00 AM. Mornings start with Emily’s 7:00 AM medications, let out the dog, then coffee and a light breakfast while she checks her seizure-tracking app and looks over the day’s plans. They’ve set up the apartment with small safety measures: a shower chair and nonslip mat in the bathroom, covers on sharp furniture corners, and a rule that Emily doesn’t cook on the stovetop when she’s alone if she’s feeling off or has had an aura recently. On workdays, she takes the COTA bus line down High Street instead of driving—she hasn’t been medically cleared to drive since a tonic–clonic seizure in early 2024—and she carries a laminated card in her wallet explaining that she has epilepsy and what to do if she has a seizure.

Evenings are quieter. Hannah usually gets home by 5:30 or 6:00 PM, and they often walk Oscar together along the neighborhood streets or in Whetstone Park if the weather is good. They meal prep simple dinners—stir-fries, sheet-pan chicken and vegetables, big batches of chili—because Emily’s energy drops after 4:00 PM and she’s more prone to seizures when she’s overtired or hungry. They’ve learned to recognize early signs: Emily gets unusually quiet, her eyes look distant, and she sometimes feels the rising stomach sensation before a bigger event. On those days, they keep plans flexible, and Hannah is ready to administer the intranasal midazolam if needed. The rescue medication has become less frightening over time, but the two ER visits in the last half-year still sit in the back of both of their minds. They talk openly about what to do in an emergency and have neighbors in the building who know Emily’s condition and can help if Hannah isn’t home.

Family is a major source of support, even from a distance. Emily’s parents, Mark and Linda, still live in Dayton and call several times a week. Her mom tends to worry and occasionally suggests moving back home so they can “keep an eye on her,” but Emily is determined to keep building a life in Columbus. Her younger brother, Jason, lives in Cincinnati and works in IT; he texts her memes and silly videos to keep her spirits up. Holidays are usually spent at her parents’ house, with everyone quietly coordinating to make sure Emily has a downstairs bedroom and easy access to a bathroom in case she seizes at night. They’re all aware that her condition makes long-term planning complicated—discussions about future grandchildren or Emily living completely on her own usually end with a “we’ll see” and a change of subject.

Over the past year, Emily has become more aware of the toll her seizures and medications take on her mood. She describes feeling irritable and emotionally flat at times, especially on Keppra and clobazam, and there are days when brain fog makes even simple tasks like paying bills or responding to emails feel overwhelming. She’s recently started seeing a therapist through a local counseling center who has experience with chronic illness, and they’re working on coping strategies for anxiety about having seizures in public and the grief she feels over lost independence, particularly not being able to drive. One of her biggest goals is to eventually meet the seizure-free interval required by Ohio law so she can safely and legally drive again; she knows that would open up job opportunities and make it more realistic to live alone someday.

In February 2026, Emily and Dr. Mehta are planning a follow-up visit specifically to revisit the Epidiolex option. Emily is coming prepared with questions about how well it works for focal seizures, how it might interact with clobazam, Keppra, and lamotrigine, and what side effects and blood tests to expect—she’s read that it can increase clobazam levels and affect liver function. She’s also concerned about practical issues like insurance coverage and whether they’ll need prior authorization, since she’s already tried and failed several other medications. Her hope is that if Epidiolex helps, they might be able to taper down her clobazam or Keppra dose to reduce fatigue, irritability, and the sense of being constantly sedated. She doesn’t expect a miracle cure, but she’s aiming for a realistic 50–75% reduction in seizures over the next year, especially eliminating monthly tonic–clonic events and reducing focal impaired-awareness seizures to less than one a month.

Day to day, Emily tries to carve out small pockets of normalcy and joy. On better days, she likes to browse the used bookstore on North High Street with Hannah on Saturdays, or meet a close friend, Maya, for lunch at a quiet café where they know the staff and feel comfortable explaining if Emily suddenly becomes unresponsive. She keeps a small sketchbook and sometimes draws while listening to podcasts about true crime or health. Sleep is a constant focus: she aims for a consistent 11:00 PM bedtime, avoids scrolling on her phone too late, and has blackout curtains in her bedroom to minimize nighttime awakenings. When seizures or postictal fatigue derail her plans, she’s learning to be kinder to herself, reminding herself that this is a chronic condition she didn’t choose.

Looking ahead, Emily’s concerns and hopes are intertwined. She wants to reduce the number of times she needs rescue medication and avoid further emergency room visits, to feel safer going to the grocery store alone or walking Oscar without worrying she might suddenly collapse. She dreams of gradually increasing her work hours, finishing her associate degree, and one day considering a family of her own, including understanding how medications like Epidiolex might affect future pregnancy plans. For now, her focus is on the next concrete steps: keeping her seizure diary up to date, making it to appointments, managing her energy at work, and seeing whether Epidiolex can be the tool that finally brings her closer to the level of seizure control—and quality of life—that she’s been striving for since that first seizure at 15.

form_summary.form_purpose: Patient or caregiver worksheet to document current seizure medications, seizure frequency, treatment satisfaction, and goals in order to guide a discussion about starting or adjusting therapy with EPIDIOLEX (cannabidiol).
form_summary.intended_recipient: Primarily the treating healthcare provider (e.g., neurologist, epileptologist, or pediatrician) reviewing the patient’s seizure history and expectations during a clinical visit; secondarily, it may be used by the patient or caregiver as a conversation aid when speaking with that provider.

Hi Mom,

I’ve been meaning to actually sit down and explain what’s been going on instead of just texting you bits and pieces. Things feel kind of precarious right now and I don’t want you to hear about stuff secondhand.

Work has been rough. I’m still on the Keppra 750 mg at 7 AM and 7 PM, but when I forgot my evening dose on December 14, 2024 and had that big tonic‑clonic seizure at my desk at Riverside Family Dental, Dr. Patel did a formal write‑up for “medication noncompliance” and put me on a 60‑day probation. I’m on a $75/month plan and you know I already had to borrow $500 from you in December just to keep up.

All of this is why I’m so focused on the upcoming visit about trying Epidiolex. I’m exhausted, but I’m still hopeful we can get things to a better place where I’m not constantly on the edge of losing my job or ending up back in the ER.

Love you,
Emily

- December 14, 2024: missed PM Keppra 750 mg dose → tonic‑clonic at my desk at Riverside Family Dental. Dr. Patel wrote me up for “medication noncompliance” and put me on 60‑day probation.
- January 12, 2025: focal impaired‑awareness seizure in break room, knocked over sterilization tray. Karen Lewis gave written warning, said if I have “another episode on the clock” Dr. Patel might cut my hours.
- November 5, 2022: wandering focal seizure into sterilization room, knocked over ~$1,200 CAD/CAM crown blocks → formal HR write‑up.

Ask if he can write a general letter about my epilepsy/fitness for work in case my job is at risk.

2) Mood history
- Keppra increase August 2023 → 3 weeks of passive suicidal thoughts, started weekly therapy with Dr. Melissa Grant at Riverside Mental Health Center (Thursdays 6 p.m.).
- April 2021: 3‑day psych hospitalization at OSU Harding after cluster of nocturnal tonic‑clonics + suicidal thoughts; now seeing Dr. Grant at OSU Harding outpatient every other Tuesday at 3 p.m.

3) Driving/court
- May 3, 2022 seizure‑related car accident on North High near campus → Franklin County Municipal Court 12‑month license suspension + $1,800 fines/fees (still paying). Also March 18, 2023 I‑71/Hudson seizure/fender‑bender and license suspension; Officer Daniels’ warning.

4) Money
- November 9, 2024 Riverside Methodist ER visit (two tonic‑clonics) → $3,200 bill now with Buckeye Recovery; $75/month plan; borrowed $500 from Mom in December. Ask about labs/monitoring costs + Epidiolex assistance programs.

5) Meds/other
- Clarify interactions with sertraline (started March 2023 after Feb 18 ER cluster), Nexplanon (placed March 2024 by Dr. Moreno after pregnancy scare), clobazam lockbox situation after 1/9/23 double‑dose.
- Long‑term goals: 6‑month seizure‑free stretch to reapply for license; eventually work 30 hrs/week consistently; maybe taper sertraline if mood improves.

Hey Maya,

Checking in like you asked. Today’s been… a lot.

This morning I had another one of those focal impaired‑awareness seizures at Riverside. I was alone at the front desk, checking in a patient, and apparently I froze, started lip‑smacking, and picking at my scrub top with my right hand again. It felt like losing a chunk of time. Later Dr. Nguyen said the patient complained that I seemed “high or out of it,” which is mortifying.

All of it brings up old stuff: the seizure in the stairwell at 1437 in October 2023 when I hit my head on the railing and woke up to paramedics because Mike from 3A called 911; the three‑day psych stay at OSU Harding in April 2021 after the cluster of nocturnal tonic‑clonics and suicidal thoughts; even the Grace Community Church thing where I had a brief focal seizure during the June 4, 2023 8:30 a.m. service and Pastor Allen pulled me aside later to ask if I was “stable enough” to keep helping with the 2–4‑year‑olds in the nursery. It feels like everywhere I go, my brain makes people question whether I belong there.

I’m trying to hang on to some hope about Epidiolex. Dr. Mehta and I are going to talk through it in a couple weeks. I keep thinking: if it could cut the big tonic‑clonics down from 1–2 a month to just a few a year, maybe I could eventually get to that 6‑month seizure‑free window and not feel like such a burden when you or Hannah walk me home from Café Kerouac.

Anyway, thanks for listening to me ramble. If you’re still up for it, maybe we can do a low‑key lunch at that quiet place on High this Saturday? Somewhere I won’t feel like a spectacle if I blank out for a minute.

❤️ E

Hi Dr. Moreno,

I hope it’s okay that I’m emailing; I just wanted to give you a heads‑up before my next visit and ask a couple of questions I forgot at my last OB‑GYN appointment.

My neurologist at OSU Wexner, Dr. Mehta, and I are seriously considering starting Epidiolex soon because my seizures are still pretty frequent despite Keppra, clobazam, and lamotrigine. I’m trying to think through how this might interact with the Nexplanon you placed in my left arm last March at the OSU clinic, after that pregnancy scare when my period was two weeks late. I’m still not ready to tell my parents about that, but I am really grateful you switched me off the low‑dose pill because I am not in a place where an unplanned pregnancy would be safe.

I’ve been reading that some seizure meds can affect hormonal birth control, so I’m wondering:

1) Does Epidiolex change how effective Nexplanon is, or is it more of an issue with pills? Should I be using condoms as backup while we see how things go?
2) Could Epidiolex change my bleeding pattern again? Things finally settled into a kind of predictable light/spotty rhythm after a few weird months post‑implant.

Longer term, I’m still thinking about what we talked about regarding pregnancy “someday,” and planning any med changes 6–12 months in advance so I don’t end up in a situation like my cousin Megan at Grant Medical Center in April 2021 when she had that prolonged seizure during labor. Right now my main goal is just to get my seizures better controlled and avoid more ER trips like the November 9, 2024 Riverside Methodist visit, but I want to make sure whatever we do now doesn’t close doors later.

Thank you for any guidance you can give, and for being so patient with all my questions.

Best,
Emily Carter

Subject: Why I’m so focused on this new seizure plan
Date: February 10, 2026

Hey Mom,

I wanted to try to explain why I’m putting so much hope into the Epidiolex conversation with Dr. Mehta this month.

Honestly, one of my biggest goals for seizure treatment now is to get stable enough that I can safely taper off the sertraline. You remember when Dr. Amanda Lewis at Riverside Behavioral Health started it in March 2023, after that awful depressive episode and the passive suicidal thoughts I had? That all came right after the cluster of tonic–clonic seizures in the apartment and the OSU Wexner Medical Center ER visit on February 18, 2023. I never want to end up in that place again, but I also don’t love the idea of being on an antidepressant forever if my mood and anxiety would improve with better seizure control.

So when you hear me talking about fewer tonic–clonics or trying to cut back on clobazam or Keppra, it’s not just about numbers in a seizure diary. It’s about feeling like myself again and not living in constant fear of repeating February 2023. If we can get things steady enough, Dr. Lewis and I could at least discuss a slow, supervised taper instead of assuming sertraline is permanent.

I know you worry I’m aiming too high, but this feels like a realistic long‑term goal, not some magic cure fantasy.

Love you,
Emily

Hey Maya,

Thanks again for meeting me at the café today. There’s some heavier stuff I didn’t quite get out in person.

Long term, one of my goals for seizure treatment is to have things controlled enough that I can safely consider pregnancy in a few years. I told Dr. Patel at Columbus Women’s Health that I’d like kids someday, but I’m honestly terrified after what happened to my cousin Megan at Grant Medical Center in April 2021, when she had that prolonged seizure during labor. Dr. Patel and I did some preconception counseling about my meds, especially clobazam. She said that if I’m serious about trying for a baby someday, we’d need my seizures better managed first, and then plan any medication changes at least 6–12 months in advance. That timeline makes it feel very real and very far away at the same time.

So when I talk about hoping Epidiolex might help or cutting down on rescue trips to the ER, it’s not just about convenience. It’s about getting to a place where pregnancy wouldn’t feel like repeating Megan’s story. Right now it’s all very “future Emily,” but it helps me to have that as a long‑term target instead of just thinking about the next seizure.

Thanks for letting me unload this.

Love,
Emily

Employee Accommodation / Health Update Form (Internal)

Employee Name: Emily Rose Carter
Position: Part-time Office Assistant

1. Brief health summary (employee-provided)
I have refractory focal epilepsy with occasional focal to bilateral tonic–clonic seizures. I’m followed by Dr. Arjun Mehta at OSU Wexner Medical Center and currently take levetiracetam (Keppra), clobazam (Onfi), and lamotrigine (Lamictal), with intranasal midazolam (Nayzilam) as rescue medication.

Most weeks I have 1–2 focal impaired-awareness seizures. After these, I’m usually confused and exhausted for 20–30 minutes and may need to step away from the front desk. About once or twice a month I have larger seizures that sometimes require EMS or an ER visit at OhioHealth Riverside Methodist Hospital. I do not drive and rely on COTA buses or family.

3. Current accommodations that help
- Shift schedule of 9:30 AM–3:00 PM on Mon/Wed/Fri
- Flexibility to sit down between phone bursts
- Coworkers aware of basic seizure first aid

I’m working with my neurologist on possibly adding Epidiolex to reduce seizure frequency and eventually meet the seizure‑free interval required by the Ohio BMV so I can reapply for my driver’s license. My long‑term goal is to be able to drive myself to work instead of depending on my sister Hannah for rides.

5. Emergency plan at work
If I have a seizure:
- Keep me safe from injury
- Do NOT put anything in my mouth
- If convulsions last >5 minutes, administer Nayzilam (in my bag) and call 911.
